environments.env: Technical Requirements — how to fetch, parse, store
Product Requirements — what the dashboard shows, why it matters

source.name: Open-Meteo Weather API

prd.goal: Display real-time and 7-day forecast rainfall for any Nepal district on the dashboard.

dashboard-widgets.widget: Hourly precipitation bar chart (mm)
Daily forecast card per district
Rainfall threshold alert badge (green / amber / red)

acceptance-criteria.criterion: Rainfall updates every 1 hour automatically
Alert fires when precipitation exceeds 60 mm/hr
Historical 7-day rainfall visible per district

trd.notes: Already implemented. No changes needed unless adding more districts.

source.implementation-prompt: Already done. To extend: fetch for all 77 Nepal districts using their centroids. Store lat/lon list in a districts.json file and loop through with Promise.all().

source.name: DHM Nepal Rainfall Watch
source.provider: Department of Hydrology and Meteorology, Government of Nepal

prd.goal: Pull official Nepal government rain-gauge station readings and overlay on the dashboard map. This is the ground-truth source for Nepal rainfall — more accurate than satellite-derived data for known station locations.

dashboard-widgets.widget: Nepal map with rain-gauge station pins (colour-coded by mm)
Station data table: name, district, elevation, last reading, status
Official DHM warning level indicator per station

acceptance-criteria.criterion: Show at least 100 DHM hydro-met stations on the map
Display reading timestamps so stale data is visible
Match DHM official warning thresholds: 60mm/1hr, 80mm/3hr, 100mm/6hr, 120mm/12hr, 140mm/24hr
Flag stations with no update in last 3 hours as "offline"

trd.approach: DHM does not expose a public JSON REST API. Use one of these two methods: Method A — WMS/WFS tiles from DHM GIS server (preferred, stable). Method B — Scrape the rainfall-watch-map page HTML table (fragile, fallback).

method-a.description: DHM publishes hydro-met data via a WMS GIS layer

steps.step: Browse DHM GeoPortal to find the rainfall FeatureService URL
Call /query endpoint with where=1=1 and outFields=* and f=json
Parse features[] array: each feature has station_name, district, rainfall_1hr, rainfall_24hr, latitude, longitude
Cache response in your backend, refresh every 30 min

method-b.description: Fallback: server-side scrape of the DHM rainfall table

steps.step: GET the page HTML server-side (CORS blocks browser fetch)
Parse table rows: each row = one station
Extract: Station Name | District | Elevation | 1Hr | 3Hr | 6Hr | 24Hr | Date-Time
Convert to JSON and cache in your database
Schedule scrape every 30 minutes via cron job

method-b.cors-note: DHM site blocks browser-side fetch. Must run scrape from your backend server, not the client.

storage.columns: station_id, station_name, district, lat, lon, elevation_m, rain_1hr, rain_3hr, rain_6hr, rain_12hr, rain_24hr, fetched_at

source.implementation-prompt: Write a Node.js/Express backend route GET /api/dhm-rainfall that: 1. Fetches https://geoportal.dhm.gov.np/server/rest/services rainfall FeatureServer. 2. Falls back to scraping https://www.dhm.gov.np/hydrology/rainfall-watch-map using axios + cheerio if GeoPortal fails. 3. Returns JSON array of stations with fields: station_name, district, lat, lon, rain_1hr, rain_3hr, rain_24hr, warning_level. 4. Calculates warning_level as "safe" | "watch" | "warning" | "danger" based on DHM official thresholds. 5. Caches result for 30 minutes to avoid hammering DHM servers. Frontend: render stations as circle markers on a Leaflet/Mapbox map coloured by warning_level.

source.name: NASA COOLR — Global Landslide Catalog
source.auth: NASA Earthdata account (free registration at urs.earthdata.nasa.gov)

prd.goal: Show historical landslide events in Nepal on the dashboard map. This gives users context: which areas have had past events, how often, and what rainfall triggered them — essential for risk communication.

dashboard-widgets.widget: Historical landslide heatmap overlaid on Nepal terrain map
Timeline chart: landslide events per year in Nepal (2007–present)
Popup on each event point: date, location, fatalities, trigger (rainfall/earthquake)
Monsoon season filter: Jun–Sep highlights

acceptance-criteria.criterion: Filter catalog to Nepal bounding box only
Show event count, total fatalities per district as a choropleth
Allow filtering by year range, trigger type, severity
Link each event to source URL / news report if available

trd.approach: Two access methods: ArcGIS REST API (live query) or static CSV download (batch load).

method-a.description: Live query via NASA ArcGIS FeatureServer
method-a.notes: May require NASA Earthdata Bearer token in Authorization header

method-b.description: Download full catalog CSV once and filter locally (no auth needed)
method-b.load-strategy: Import into your database on first run; refresh monthly

key-fields.field: landslide_type — debris flow, rockfall, mudslide, etc.

storage.columns: event_id, event_date, lat, lon, district, province, type, fatalities, injuries, trigger, source_url, imported_at
storage.index: CREATE INDEX ON landslide_catalog (lat, lon); CREATE INDEX ON landslide_catalog (event_date);

source.implementation-prompt: Write a data ingestion script (Python or Node.js) that: 1. Downloads the NASA COOLR CSV from the landslide viewer download link. 2. Filters rows where country_name = "Nepal" (approx 300–500 events). 3. Geocodes missing district names using lat/lon against Nepal admin boundary GeoJSON (from geojson-nepal npm package or HDX dataset). 4. Inserts into PostgreSQL table landslide_catalog with upsert on event_id. 5. Creates a GET /api/catalog endpoint that returns events filtered by: - bbox (lat_min, lat_max, lon_min, lon_max) - year_from, year_to - trigger (rainfall | earthquake | all) 6. Frontend: render as clustered CircleMarkers on Leaflet; red = fatalities > 5, orange = 1-5, yellow = 0. Run this script on deployment and add a monthly cron to refresh.

source.name: BIPAD Portal Nepal
source.provider: NDRRMA — National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Authority, Gov. of Nepal

auth.registration: Request at bipadportal.gov.np — contact NDRRMA for developer access

prd.goal: Pull live and historical disaster events (floods, landslides) from Nepal's official government disaster information system. This is the most authoritative Nepal-specific source for disaster incident records, loss data, and real-time alerts.

dashboard-widgets.widget: Real-time alert feed: latest disaster events in Nepal (last 7 days)
District-level incident count map (choropleth)
Loss table: deaths, missing, injured, families affected per event
Monsoon season summary card

acceptance-criteria.criterion: Show events filtered to hazard_type = "landslide" and "flood"
Display district name, date, casualties, affected families
Alert badge when a new event is reported in the last 24 hours
Link to official BIPAD event URL for details

trd.common-issue: The BIPAD API requires a Bearer token that expires and must be refreshed. Most "not working" issues are due to: expired token, wrong header format, or using the wrong API version (v1 vs v2).

endpoint.response-fields: id, title, hazard, district, loss_human_death, loss_human_missing, loss_human_injured, loss_family_affected, incident_on, verified

endpoint.notes: Real-time module for live sensor / weather station feeds

debugging-steps.step: If 401 → token expired. Re-login via POST /api/v1/auth/login/ with username+password to get a new token.
If 403 → your account doesn't have API access. Email NDRRMA to enable it.
If CORS error → the BIPAD API does NOT support browser-side fetch. Must proxy through your backend.
Check Accept header: set Accept: application/json explicitly.

backend-proxy.description: Create a backend proxy to avoid CORS and hide your token
backend-proxy.implementation: Your server calls bipadportal.gov.np with the token in headers. Returns filtered JSON to your frontend. Cache responses for 15 minutes.

token-refresh.strategy: Store token in environment variable. On 401, auto-refresh token and retry once.

storage.columns: bipad_id, title, hazard, district_id, district_name, province, lat, lon, deaths, missing, injured, families_affected, incident_date, verified, source_url, fetched_at

trd.polling-interval: 15 minutes for alerts; 6 hours for historical incidents

source.implementation-prompt: Fix the broken BIPAD integration with this approach: 1. Backend: create a token store — on server start, POST to /api/v1/auth/login/ and store the token. 2. Create an axios instance with baseURL=https://bipadportal.gov.np and Authorization header set. 3. Add a response interceptor: on 401, refresh token and retry the original request once. 4. Create these backend routes (all proxied through your server to avoid CORS): - GET /api/bipad/incidents?hazard=landslide&days=30 → returns last 30 days of landslide events - GET /api/bipad/alerts → returns active alerts - GET /api/bipad/summary → returns death/missing/injured totals for current monsoon season 5. Frontend: poll /api/bipad/alerts every 5 minutes; show a notification banner if new events appear. 6. Add error state: if BIPAD is unreachable, show "BIPAD data unavailable" badge and use cached data. Test with: curl -X POST https://bipadportal.gov.np/api/v1/auth/login/ -d '{"username":"X","password":"Y"}' -H "Content-Type: application/json"

source.name: Copernicus DEM / SRTM Digital Elevation Model
source.provider: ESA Copernicus / NASA SRTM via OpenTopography

auth.header: API_Key query parameter or Authorization header

prd.goal: Provide slope, aspect, and elevation layers as static background context on the dashboard map. Slope angle is the most critical static factor for landslide susceptibility — steep slopes (greater than 30°) dramatically increase risk when combined with heavy rainfall.

dashboard-widgets.widget: Hillshade terrain base layer on the map (replaces flat map tiles)
Slope angle overlay (toggle on/off): green = flat, orange = moderate, red = steep
Elevation profile chart when user draws a line on the map
Susceptibility index layer computed from slope + soil + land-cover

acceptance-criteria.criterion: DEM data covers all 77 Nepal districts
Slope layer pre-computed and served as raster tiles (not fetched live)
Hillshade renders correctly at zoom levels 8–14
Elevation shown in metres in map tooltips

trd.approach: DEM is a one-time download, not a live API. Download once, process into tiles, serve from your own tile server or CDN. Do NOT fetch live per request — files are large.

option.name: OpenTopography REST API (recommended for targeted bbox)
Copernicus DEM on AWS S3 (free, no auth)
option.notes: Download only the tiles covering Nepal (N26–N30, E080–E088). About 24 tiles.

processing-pipeline.step: Download Nepal DEM tiles using AWS CLI (no auth required)
Merge tiles: gdal_merge.py -o nepal_dem.tif tile1.tif tile2.tif ...
Compute slope: gdaldem slope nepal_dem.tif nepal_slope.tif
Compute hillshade: gdaldem hillshade nepal_dem.tif nepal_hillshade.tif
Convert to Cloud Optimised GeoTIFF: gdal_translate -of COG nepal_slope.tif nepal_slope_cog.tif
Serve tiles from your server or upload to S3/Cloudflare R2

trd.tools-required: GDAL (gdal-bin), Python with rasterio, tippecanoe (optional for vector tiles)

frontend-integration.library: Leaflet with L.tileLayer or MapboxGL raster-dem source

trd.refresh-strategy: Static — DEM does not change. Re-download only if a major earthquake changes terrain. Re-download recommendation: after Mw 6.0+ earthquake in Nepal.

source.implementation-prompt: Set up DEM processing pipeline: 1. Install GDAL: sudo apt-get install gdal-bin python3-gdal 2. Download Nepal Copernicus DEM tiles from AWS S3 (no auth): aws s3 sync s3://copernicus-dem-30m/ ./dem-tiles/ --exclude "*" --include "*/N2*E08*" --no-sign-request 3. Run: gdal_merge.py -o nepal_dem.tif dem-tiles/**/*.tif 4. Run: gdaldem slope nepal_dem.tif nepal_slope.tif -compute_edges 5. Run: gdaldem hillshade nepal_dem.tif nepal_hillshade.tif -z 2 -s 111120 6. Classify slope into risk zones and colorize: gdaldem color-relief nepal_slope.tif slope_colors.txt nepal_slope_colored.tif (slope_colors.txt: 0 green, 15 yellow, 30 orange, 45 red, 90 darkred) 7. Generate tiles: gdal2tiles.py --zoom=7-13 --processes=4 nepal_slope_colored.tif ./public/tiles/slope/ 8. In your Leaflet map: L.tileLayer('/tiles/slope/{z}/{x}/{y}.png', {opacity: 0.5}).addTo(map) 9. Add a layer toggle button so users can turn slope overlay on/off.

source.name: Sentinel-1 SAR — Ground Motion Detection

auth.registration: Free at urs.earthdata.nasa.gov — then enable ASF DAAC app

prd.goal: Detect ground surface deformation (slope creep, land movement) before and after a landslide event using SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite images. This is the most advanced layer — it adds predictive power by spotting unstable slopes before they fail.

dashboard-widgets.widget: Ground deformation map overlay: mm/year displacement per pixel (InSAR)
Change detection alert: areas with sudden deformation spike in last 12-day SAR pass
Pre/post event image comparison slider for confirmed landslide locations
SAR acquisition calendar: when was the last satellite pass over each district?

acceptance-criteria.criterion: Show InSAR displacement layer for Nepal on the dashboard map
Alert when a new SAR pass shows deformation greater than 20mm in a high-risk zone
Data updates every 12 days (Sentinel-1 revisit time over Nepal)
Graceful degradation: if SAR unavailable, show "last pass" date and cached layer

trd.approach: SAR processing (InSAR) is computationally heavy. Use one of: Option A — ASF HyP3 (cloud-processed InSAR, recommended, no GPU needed). Option B — Google Earth Engine (hosted processing, free quota). Option C — Download raw Sentinel-1 SLC and process locally with SNAP (advanced, slow).

option-a.name: ASF HyP3 On-Demand InSAR Processing
option-a.description: Submit Sentinel-1 scene pairs to ASF HyP3 API. HyP3 runs GAMMA InSAR processing in the cloud. You get back a GeoTIFF of displacement (mm) within hours.

steps.step: Register at urs.earthdata.nasa.gov and enable ASF DAAC app
Search for Sentinel-1 scenes over Nepal using ASF Vertex or Search API
Submit an InSAR job for a scene pair (reference + secondary image, 12 days apart)
Poll job status; download output GeoTIFF when complete
Display displacement raster on dashboard map

call.description: When SUCCEEDED, download .files[].url — a GeoTIFF zip

option-b.name: Google Earth Engine (alternative)
option-b.description: Run InSAR or change detection directly in GEE using Sentinel-1 GRD images.
option-b.output: Export to Google Drive as GeoTIFF, then serve as map tile
option-b.notes: GEE does not do full InSAR but detects backscatter change useful for post-event mapping

storage.columns: job_id, scene_reference, scene_secondary, acquisition_date, product_url, displacement_tif_path, coherence_tif_path, processed_at, status

trd.schedule: Sentinel-1 passes over Nepal approximately every 12 days. Schedule a job submission every 12 days aligned to the satellite pass schedule. Monitor: https://sentinels.copernicus.eu/web/sentinel/missions/sentinel-1/observation-scenario

source.implementation-prompt: Implement SAR integration using ASF HyP3 in these phases: PHASE 1 — Scene Search (Week 1): Write a script that calls the ASF Search API to find the 2 most recent Sentinel-1 SLC scenes over Nepal (12 days apart, same relative orbit). Store scene IDs in your database. PHASE 2 — Job Submission (Week 1-2): POST to https://hyp3-api.asf.alaska.edu/jobs with INSAR_GAMMA job type. Store job_id and poll /jobs/{job_id} every 10 minutes until status = SUCCEEDED. Download the _displacement.tif file when done. PHASE 3 — Tile Generation (Week 2): Convert displacement GeoTIFF to colourised map tiles using gdal2tiles.py. Positive values (uplift) = blue, negative (subsidence/slide) = red. Overlay on the dashboard Leaflet map as a semi-transparent tile layer. PHASE 4 — Alerting (Week 3): Compute zonal statistics for each district: mean displacement from latest InSAR. If mean displacement in a district exceeds 20mm downslope, trigger an alert. Store alert in database and push to dashboard notification panel. Test first with a single Sentinel-1 pair over Sindhupalchowk district (high landslide history) before scaling to all Nepal.

backend-stack.layer: Node.js / Express — single entry point for all data proxying
One service module per source (dhm.service.js, bipad.service.js, etc.)
Redis — 15min TTL for live feeds, 24hr for catalogs
PostgreSQL with PostGIS extension for spatial queries
Bull / node-cron for scheduled fetches and HyP3 polling
TilesServer or nginx serving static gdal2tiles output

frontend-stack.layer: Leaflet.js or MapboxGL — base map + overlays
Chart.js or Recharts — rainfall timelines, event counts
React context or Zustand — live data subscriptions
WebSocket or SSE — push new BIPAD alerts to all connected dashboards

data-flow.flow: Open-Meteo API → /api/weather → Frontend rainfall chart DHM Nepal (scrape) → /api/dhm-rainfall → Map station pins
Copernicus DEM (pre-processed) → slope tiles → Map slope overlay NASA SMAP soil moisture → /api/soil → Risk calculation LHASA FeatureServer → /api/lhasa → Hazard nowcast layer
NASA COOLR CSV (monthly) → landslide_catalog table → /api/catalog → Map cluster markers BIPAD incidents (6hr) → bipad_incidents table → /api/bipad/incidents → Event feed
ASF Sentinel-1 search (12-day) → HyP3 InSAR job → displacement.tif → slope tiles → Map deformation layer

phase.label: Fix broken integrations
phase.task: Fix BIPAD token refresh — implement auto-refresh on 401
Implement DHM scraper backend route with cheerio
Test both with curl before connecting to frontend

phase.label: Add static layers
phase.task: Download Nepal Copernicus DEM from AWS S3
Run GDAL slope + hillshade processing pipeline
Generate and serve map tiles at zoom 7–13
Add tile layer toggle on Leaflet map

phase.label: Historical catalog
phase.task: Download NASA COOLR CSV and import Nepal events into PostgreSQL
Create /api/catalog endpoint with bbox and date filters

phase.label: SAR ground motion (advanced)
phase.task: Register for ASF Earthdata account and test HyP3 API with Postman
Build scene-search + job-submission script for one test district
Process output displacement GeoTIFF into colourised tiles
Overlay on dashboard and add displacement alert logic

phase.label: Monitoring and maintenance
phase.task: Add data freshness indicators on dashboard (last updated timestamps)
Set up uptime monitor for each data source endpoint
12-day SAR job automation aligned to Sentinel-1 pass schedule
